channel.title: TechNode
channel.description: Latest news and trends about tech in China

item.title: 10-second health checks and continuous monitoring for a new approach to personal health
item.dc:creator: Jessie Wu
item.description: As technology and healthcare increasingly converge, Chinese startup Feipu Tech is exploring how non-invasive health check technologies can become part of everyday life. By combining consumer-facing AI with computational biology models, the company aims to make health monitoring faster, more accurate, and more accessible outside traditional medical settings. Feipu’s devices are designed for simplicity and […]
item.content:encoded:
As technology and healthcare increasingly converge, Chinese startup Feipu Tech is exploring how non-invasive health check technologies can become part of everyday life. By combining consumer-facing AI with computational biology models, the company aims to make health monitoring faster, more accurate, and more accessible outside traditional medical settings.

Feipu’s devices are designed for simplicity and speed. Users can collect and analyze multiple key health indicators in seconds, with applications ranging from individual use at home to workplaces, gyms, and community health stations. This approach reflects a broader shift toward convenient, continuous health management that integrates seamlessly into daily routines.

Health checks enter everyday life with a 10-second facial scan

The Feipu non-invasive health check robot integrates multiple technologies, including multimodal optical tomography and its self-developed FEIPU MiLC (Multimodal Integrated Language Computing) engine. According to the company, the device can complete non-invasive measurements of key physiological indicators—such as blood pressure, blood glucose, blood lipids, hemoglobin, and BMI—in about 10 seconds.

The device is designed for simple, self-service use. A user stands naturally in front of the robot, which is about the size of a TV screen, aligns their face with the scanning area, and the system automatically collects facial and subcutaneous optical signals. The AI engine processes this data in real time and generates a personalized report, which includes key health indicators, risk alerts, and lifestyle recommendations.

The company reports that the data shows an overall accuracy rate of over 85%, with some indicators (such as blood pressure and blood glucose) reaching around 95%, approaching the performance of certain Class II medical devices of China. However, its real-world performance still requires further validation through independent studies and long-term data.

Compared with traditional health check procedures, this device does not involve blood draws or complex operations. Users can complete the test simply by standing in front of the device, while the system models their physiological state through facial scanning and subcutaneous optical signal analysis.

A health ecosystem from homes to communities

The Feipu robot is designed for versatility and adaptability. Its lightweight body, about the size of a 32-inch TV, makes it suitable for homes, gyms, workplaces, senior care facilities, and community health stations.

At home, elderly family members and children can perform self-checks, with data synced to the cloud to create long-term health records. In workplaces, it provides basic health monitoring to track employee well-being and support occupational health management.

In community or public service settings, the device can be used in partnership with relevant institutions, allowing residents to conduct self-service health screenings and contribute data to public health platforms.

Life mirror platform and health technology exploration

The company has developed the Human Life Mirror computing platform, which integrates multimodal sensing, deep reinforcement learning, and large-scale biological data algorithms. This allows the device to continuously optimize its performance based on usage data, as terminal-collected information feeds back into the AI model and improves detection accuracy over time.

Building on this platform, the company is also exploring applications such as early cancer screening, immune system assessment, and auxiliary diagnostic robots. It has launched the company’s first genome-scale SNP analysis model, SNPBag, to support large-scale biological data analysis and research.

Social impact of making health management natural

Feipu Tech aims to make health monitoring as simple as checking the weather. As the company’s founder, Tang Kun, puts it, “We are using technology to give everyone the ability to become a life scientist.”

Under the Healthy China initiative, Feipu Tech is leveraging non-invasive health check technology to tap into the trillion-yuan health management market. The closed-loop system of “detection, alert, and intervention” provides a reference point for industry practices and applies life science research findings in everyday settings.

item.title: Alibaba reports 36% cloud revenue growth, highlights AI momentum in December quarter
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Alibaba said its Cloud Intelligence Group revenue rose 36% year-over-year to RMB 43.28 billion ($6.19 billion) in the December quarter, with AI-related product revenue posting triple-digit growth for the tenth consecutive quarter. Excluding disposed businesses, the group’s total revenue grew 9% year-over-year on a like-for-like basis to RMB 284.84 billion ($40.73 billion). The company also […]
item.content:encoded:
Alibaba said its Cloud Intelligence Group revenue rose 36% year-over-year to RMB 43.28 billion ($6.19 billion) in the December quarter, with AI-related product revenue posting triple-digit growth for the tenth consecutive quarter. Excluding disposed businesses, the group’s total revenue grew 9% year-over-year on a like-for-like basis to RMB 284.84 billion ($40.73 billion).

The company also highlighted stronger consumer AI adoption, saying the Qwen App surpassed 300 million monthly active users across all platforms as of February. Alibaba said its quick commerce business grew 56% during the quarter, while Taobao Instant Commerce has been integrated into the Qwen App to support delivery orders through conversational AI.

Alibaba said it is continuing to scale investment in AI and cloud infrastructure, while advancing organizational changes tied to its full-stack AI strategy across models, chips, cloud, and applications. [TechNode reporting]

item.title: Xiaomi unveils new-gen SU7 as an updated model in its EV lineup
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Xiaomi on Thursday held its spring product launch event, where it unveiled the new-generation Xiaomi SU7, positioning the model as an upgraded offering in its electric vehicle lineup. The new SU7 introduces updates across safety, driving performance and in-car technology, according to the company. It comes equipped with Xiaomi’s V6s Plus motor and a silicon […]
item.content:encoded:
Xiaomi on Thursday held its spring product launch event, where it unveiled the new-generation Xiaomi SU7, positioning the model as an upgraded offering in its electric vehicle lineup. The new SU7 introduces updates across safety, driving performance and in-car technology, according to the company. It comes equipped with Xiaomi’s V6s Plus motor and a silicon carbide-based high-voltage platform, while the Pro version offers a claimed range of up to 902 km. The Max variant supports fast charging, adding up to 670 km of range in 15 minutes under certain conditions.

Xiaomi said the SU7 Max can accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h in 3.08 seconds, with a top speed of 265 km/h. On safety, the vehicle uses 2200 MPa-strength steel in parts of the body structure and includes nine airbags and a redundant door handle system. Pricing starts at 219,900 yuan ($30,800), slightly higher than the previous generation. Xiaomi CEO Lei Jun said the upgrades justify the increase. [TechNode Reporting]

item.title: Tech Odyssey Series: They 3D-Printed Every Employee’s Ears to Make 3D Audio Feel More Real
item.dc:creator: TechNode Staff
item.description: In the previous episode, we looked at how Bhout turned one founder’s dream into a business. This time, we turn to a lower-profile but technically rigorous company: Sound Particles. When audiences hear the roar of a sandworm in Dune or feel the force of a blast wave in Oppenheimer, few would imagine that part of […]
item.content:encoded:
In the previous episode, we looked at how Bhout turned one founder’s dream into a business. This time, we turn to a lower-profile but technically rigorous company: Sound Particles.

When audiences hear the roar of a sandworm in Dune or feel the force of a blast wave in Oppenheimer, few would imagine that part of the technology behind those immersive soundscapes comes from a 17-person company based in Lisbon, Portugal. Founded in 2016, Sound Particles has earned its place in the high-end film, game, and music production pipeline by bringing a core idea from computer graphics into audio.

Its core, genre-redefining concept is to treat individual sounds like particles in a 3D environment. Instead of building complex soundscapes one layer at a time, creators can place sounds in virtual space, give them defined position and movement, and shape the final listening perspective through virtual microphones. That fundamentally changes how immersive audio is made. During our visit, the team showed how a small number of base samples could be adjusted into thousands of distinct variations, making dense, complex scenes feel far more natural, while reducing the need for endless manual edits.

What stood out to us most during our visit, even more than the core technology itself, was the team’s focus on acoustic realism. In its office, rows of 3D-printed ears, heads, and torsos—all modeled on the company’s employees—are used for granular acoustic testing. The logic is simple: every person’s ears are structurally unique, and sound is shaped not only by where it comes from, but also by how it interacts with each listener’s ears and body. That is why the team has gone beyond printing ears alone, and started recreating full heads and torsos to capture even more nuanced acoustic details.

This work connects directly to the company’s biggest next ambition. Traditional immersive audio depends heavily on multi-speaker theater setups, but most people listen to audio through everyday headphones. Sound Particles has spent years developing personalized binaural audio technology, using the unique shape of a listener’s head and ears to simulate how sound travels and interacts with the body. The goal is to bring a more convincing, theater-quality 3D audio experience to ordinary users, no room full of speakers required.

The company has also expanded far beyond professional audio tools. Its product lineup now includes plugins for digital audio workstations, industry-first immersive synthesizers, and a dedicated 3D sound library. At the same time, it has started applying the same spatial audio technology to artificial intelligence, generating audio datasets with precise spatial labels and realistic environmental noise to train neural networks for speech recognition, smart vehicle environment detection, and related systems.

What makes Sound Particles interesting is not just the market it has entered, but the way it has grown. It started with one difficult, foundational question—how sound should exist in 3D space—and kept pushing that question further, from film production to consumer headphones, and even into AI training. For a small team based in Lisbon, that quiet, consistent focus on a single core question has already taken them a long way.

item.title: NetEase denies reports of replacing all outsourced workers with AI
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: NetEase said reports that it plans to replace all outsourced workers with AI are untrue. The company said recent personnel changes stem from routine business adjustments and staff turnover across certain projects, including the optimization of some outsourced roles involving basic skill sets, as part of normal operations. The adjustments will not affect overall operations […]
item.content:encoded: NetEase said reports that it plans to replace all outsourced workers with AI are untrue. The company said recent personnel changes stem from routine business adjustments and staff turnover across certain projects, including the optimization of some outsourced roles involving basic skill sets, as part of normal operations. The adjustments will not affect overall operations or the normal functioning of its business units. While AI has been deployed in some business scenarios, the technology cannot fully replace roles requiring complex execution and human judgment. [IThome, in Chinese]

item.title: Pop Mart teams up with Sony Pictures to develop live-action LABUBU film
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Pop Mart today said it will develop a live-action/CGI film based on its LABUBU character in partnership with Sony Pictures. The project, centered on the company’s LABUBU IP, is in early development and will combine live-action footage with computer-generated imagery. Director Paul King, known for the Paddington films and Wonka, will direct and produce the […]
item.content:encoded: Pop Mart today said it will develop a live-action/CGI film based on its LABUBU character in partnership with Sony Pictures. The project, centered on the company’s LABUBU IP, is in early development and will combine live-action footage with computer-generated imagery. Director Paul King, known for the Paddington films and Wonka, will direct and produce the project. Long Jiasheng, creator of LABUBU, will be involved in the film’s creative development. King will co-write the screenplay with Simon Farnaby. [TechNode Reporting]

item.title: ByteDance issues internal OpenClaw security rules, launches ByteClaw
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: ByteDance’s security team has issued internal security guidelines for OpenClaw and launched an enterprise service called ByteClaw for employees, according to Sina Tech. Built on Volcano Engine’s ArkClaw enterprise version, ByteClaw supports unified authentication, access control, and permission management. The guidelines flagged five key risks tied to OpenClaw-style tools, including prompt injection, sensitive data theft, […]
item.content:encoded:
ByteDance’s security team has issued internal security guidelines for OpenClaw and launched an enterprise service called ByteClaw for employees, according to Sina Tech. Built on Volcano Engine’s ArkClaw enterprise version, ByteClaw supports unified authentication, access control, and permission management.

The guidelines flagged five key risks tied to OpenClaw-style tools, including prompt injection, sensitive data theft, supply chain vulnerabilities, misconfigured access control, and malicious plugins. Employees are told to prioritize compliant tools such as ByteClaw and avoid deploying such tools in core production environments. [Sina Tech, in Chinese]

item.title: Alibaba Cloud raises AI computing and storage prices by up to 34% amid surging demand
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: Alibaba Cloud said it has raised prices for its AI computing and storage products by up to 34%, citing surging global demand for AI and higher supply chain costs, according to a notice on its website. The price increases affect a range of offerings, including AI accelerator cards such as the T-Head Zhenwu 810E, which […]
item.content:encoded:
Alibaba Cloud said it has raised prices for its AI computing and storage products by up to 34%, citing surging global demand for AI and higher supply chain costs, according to a notice on its website. The price increases affect a range of offerings, including AI accelerator cards such as the T-Head Zhenwu 810E, which have risen between 5% and 34%. Its file storage product CPFS (AI computing edition) has increased by about 30%.

People familiar with the matter said a sharp rise in token usage has also driven the adjustments. Alibaba Cloud Bailian, the company’s Model-as-a-Service platform, recorded its fastest growth on record in the first quarter. Alibaba Cloud is reallocating limited AI computing resources toward token-based services. [Cailian Press, in Chinese]

item.title: ByteDance Reportedly Delays Doubao AI Glasses Launch Plan
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: ByteDance has reportedly delayed the production plan for its Doubao AI glasses project, and the first-generation product is now unlikely to reach the market, according to supply chain sources cited by Blue Whale Tech. One person familiar with the matter said the company is still likely to pursue AI glasses in the future, partly because […]
item.content:encoded:
ByteDance has reportedly delayed the production plan for its Doubao AI glasses project, and the first-generation product is now unlikely to reach the market, according to supply chain sources cited by Blue Whale Tech.

One person familiar with the matter said the company is still likely to pursue AI glasses in the future, partly because Qualcomm’s AR1 chip has already been procured. The person said the next phase may not begin until the market reaches a clearer inflection point and products can show stronger differentiation and broader consumer appeal.

The report added that ByteDance applies strict internal standards to AI hardware, especially on product differentiation. The current plan was reportedly pushed back because the product was not seen as sufficiently distinct from existing offerings on the market. [Blue Whale Finance, in Chinese]

item.title: DJI car tech unit spinout ZYT is raising pre-IPO funding, report says
item.dc:creator: TechNode Feed
item.description: ZYT, also known as Zhuoyu Technology, is raising a pre-IPO round of about RMB 2 billion ($280 million) at a pre-money valuation of around RMB 12.5 billion ($1.74 billion), according to EqualOcean Auto. The report added that the company is also planning a Hong Kong listing this year. The company was originally established in 2016 […]
item.content:encoded:
ZYT, also known as Zhuoyu Technology, is raising a pre-IPO round of about RMB 2 billion ($280 million) at a pre-money valuation of around RMB 12.5 billion ($1.74 billion), according to EqualOcean Auto. The report added that the company is also planning a Hong Kong listing this year.

The company was originally established in 2016 as Chinese drone maker DJI’s automotive subsidiary focused on intelligent driving technology. It was spun out from DJI in 2023 and formally adopted the Zhuoyu brand in June 2024. In November 2025, Zhuoyu said it had received more than RMB 3.6 billion in strategic investment from FAW Group. [EqualOcean Auto, in Chinese]
